description: High-volume, fixed-venue attractions — museums, nature parks, cultural sites, zoos. No capacity constraint means the priority is streamlined check-in and wide distribution, not slot management.
key_characteristics: High booking volume, low per-booking complexity
No capacity constraint — can serve as many visitors as arrive
Streamlined check-in at scale is operationally critical
Wide distribution strategy — visibility on as many channels as possible
pain_points: Check-in is slow or inconsistent — scanning tickets from OTAs, walk-ins, and direct bookings separately
Managing multiple distribution channels (OTAs, direct, resellers) without a centralised inventory view
No visibility into which channels are driving bookings — hard to optimise marketing spend
No upsell or add-on capability at the point of booking (guided tours, audio guides, F&B)
Peak season creates queue and check-in bottlenecks that damage guest experience
general_discovery_questions: What does your check-in process look like at peak times — do you scan tickets from each booking source separately?
How many distribution channels are you on currently, and how do you manage inventory across them?
Do you have visibility into which channels are driving the most bookings, or is that hard to track?
Are you doing anything at the point of booking to upsell guided tours or add-ons?
fareharbor_strengths: Unified check-in across all booking sources — single scan regardless of channel
Channel manager for centralised distribution across OTAs and direct
Built-in upsell and add-on capability at checkout
